topic: Human recruiter reads and fixes resume bullets with visible score rubric

youtube.title: Human recruiter reads and fixes resume bullets with visible score rubric | Resume Teardown
youtube.description: A funny recruiter-style teardown of why a qualified resume can be hard to find, how to translate a job description into resume proof, and where Signal fits without fake claims.

script.text: Hey, Job Hunters! Ever feel like your resume is a black hole of vague buzzwords? Like, who wrote this, a corporate chatbot?
script.visuals: Show text animations of terrible resume words floating away in space.

script.text: Welcome to the Resume Crime Scene! Today, we’re solving the mystery of job description matches.
script.visuals: Open up an 'investigation' scene, complete with magnifying glasses and CSI-style music.

script.text: <INSERT BULLET FROM VIEWER SUBMITTED RESUME> - This sounds like you're merely existing at your job. Pro tip: Be specific! Instead of 'Responsible for project management,' try something like, 'Led a team of 5 to successfully complete project X under budget and two weeks early!'
script.visuals: Screen split with the original bullet on one side and the improved bullet on the other side.

script.text: Guys, unless your resume is written by Shakespeare, stop using flowery language. Don't tell me you're 'utilizing interpersonal skills' – we want results, not a sonnet!
script.visuals: Roast-style reactions with dramatic sound effects for every word that sounds too fancy.

script.text: Now, remember to grab your free Signal score to ensure your resume hits the mark! Because who needs auto-rejects, am I right?
script.visuals: Show up scrolling through ‘Signal score’ graphics with multiple success stories.

script.text: Don’t forget to like, subscribe, and comment to submit your resume for next week's roast! See you soon!
script.visuals: End with upbeat music and flashy animations encouraging users to engage.

youtube.seoTitle: Human recruiter reads and fixes resume bullets with visible score rubric | Resume Teardown with Signal by ATSHacker
youtube.cta: Paste the job description and check your free Signal score before you apply.

scriptSections.label: Cold open
scriptSections.script: Human recruiter reads and fixes resume bullets with visible score rubric This resume looks professional, which is why the mistake is dangerous. On screen, it feels polished. In a recruiter search, the proof is still hard to find.
scriptSections.visual: Cold open with comic red stamp, quick zoom, Signal mascot reaction, and resume/JD split screen.

scriptSections.label: Open loop
scriptSections.script: We are not adding fake experience. We are checking whether the resume explains the real experience in the same language as the target job.
scriptSections.visual: Show the weak resume score, then hold the improved score as the unresolved payoff.

scriptSections.label: Translate the job description
scriptSections.script: Before touching the resume, pull out the role title, tools, responsibilities, metrics, and proof signals the job actually asks for.
scriptSections.visual: Resume on left, job description on right, mismatched terms highlighted with funny labels.

scriptSections.label: Recruiter search test
scriptSections.script: If a recruiter searches for the first three important terms from the job description, this resume should produce useful hits. Right now, the source bullet is too broad.
scriptSections.visual: Search console typing the first three job-description terms and showing weak matches.

scriptSections.label: Resume crime scene
scriptSections.script: Now compare the resume line by line. The weak bullet is not dishonest. It is just too vague to carry the role-specific proof.

scriptSections.label: Why it gets missed
scriptSections.script: A polished sentence can still hide the most important evidence. Recruiters skim for tools, scope, and outcomes because those make fit easy to verify.
scriptSections.visual: Zoom into the weak bullet and place missing proof labels beside it.

scriptSections.label: Live bullet fix
scriptSections.script: The fix is to say the real work clearly: what tool, what scope, what audience, and what result. No fake claims, just usable evidence.

scriptSections.label: Score reveal
scriptSections.script: Once the evidence matches the role language, the example score moves up. The point is not gaming a robot. The point is making real fit easier to see.
scriptSections.visual: Score meter jumps from low match to high match with before/after resume panels.

scriptSections.label: Trust and CTA
scriptSections.script: Signal automates this comparison. Paste the job description, upload the resume, and check the free score first. If the gap is real, then the paid resume and cover letter package makes sense.
scriptSections.visual: Free Signal score page, trust badges, and bundle CTA.

shorts.title: I would circle this line first
shorts.hook: I would circle this line first.
shorts.script: Okay, circle this line: Created reports for business teams and leadership. Normal sentence, but I cannot see SQL, Tableau, or a result. So the rubric gives it 32, not 89. Rewrite it: built a SQL churn dashboard that saved six hours a week and flagged 18 at-risk accounts. Now I see the tool, scope, and outcome. That is why it moves to 89. Run the free Signal score before you apply.
storyboard: Case: Data Analyst Resume against Product Data Analyst.
Open on the resume as if a reviewer is reading it at a desk.
Circle the exact weak line: Created reports for business teams and leadership.
Place the job description beside it with SQL, Tableau, and cohort analysis highlighted.
Show the Signal Fit Score rubric before the score reveal.
Rewrite the line in-place using only the real work.
Reveal the score after the low-score rationale is visible.

props.hook1: This bullet has a high chance of synergy and zero proof
props.subline: Signal compares resume language to the target job.
props.subhook: The line sounds normal until you compare it to the job.

Worked with data to help teams understand customer trends.
Helped maintain dashboards for weekly performance meetings.
props.beforeBullet: Created reports for business teams and leadership.
props.afterBullet: Built SQL churn dashboard in Tableau that cut weekly reporting by 6 hours and flagged 18 at-risk customer accounts.

score_rubric.label: Signal Fit Score
score_rubric.beforeExplanation: The original line forces the reviewer to infer tools, scope, and outcome.
score_rubric.afterExplanation: The rewrite puts the job terms, metric proof, and outcome on the page.

scoreRubric.label: Signal Fit Score
scoreRubric.beforeExplanation: The original line forces the reviewer to infer tools, scope, and outcome.
scoreRubric.afterExplanation: The rewrite puts the job terms, metric proof, and outcome on the page.

humanReadBeats.text: Read the exact resume line: Created reports for business teams and leadership.
I believe the work happened; I just cannot see the proof yet.
Compare it to the job requirement: SQL, Tableau, cohort analysis.
Explain the visible rubric before the score: 32/100.
Rewrite only the same experience: built a SQL churn dashboard that saved six hours a week and flagged 18 at-risk accounts.

props.problemPunchline: The work may be real. The evidence is not readable.
props.teardownPunchline: Low score reason: the rubric cannot find tool, metric, or result.
props.fixPunchline: A recruiter should not have to infer the proof.

Translate work into measurable outcomes tied to cohort analysis.
Partner cross-functionally and communicate progress clearly to stakeholders.

props.voiceover_text: Okay, circle this line: Created reports for business teams and leadership. Normal sentence, but I cannot see SQL, Tableau, or a result. So the rubric gives it 32, not 89. Rewrite it: built a SQL churn dashboard that saved six hours a week and flagged 18 at-risk accounts. Now I see the tool, scope, and outcome. That is why it moves to 89. Run the free Signal score before you apply.

shorts.title: I searched the resume. Bad news.
shorts.hook: I searched the resume. Bad news.
shorts.script: Here is my Ctrl F test. I search Salesforce. Nothing useful. pipeline generation? Still thin. Then I read: Responsible for sales opportunities in assigned territory. Real work, but I am guessing. So the rubric starts at 37. I would rewrite it: generated 1.8 million in qualified Salesforce pipeline. Now the search terms and proof are on the page. That is why it moves to 88. Run the free Signal score before you apply.
storyboard: Case: Account Executive Resume against Mid-Market Account Executive.
Open on a reviewer search box over the resume, searching for Salesforce.
Show the weak bullet that does not include the searched language.
Search pipeline generation and show the miss again.
Show the Signal Fit Score rubric before the score reveal.
Rewrite the bullet and rerun the search with a visible match.
Close after the low-score rationale explains the jump.

props.afterBullet: Generated $1.8M in qualified Salesforce pipeline through 42 monthly discovery calls and MEDDICC-based account prioritization.

humanReadBeats.text: Read the exact resume line: Responsible for sales opportunities in assigned territory.
The sales work may be there; the pipeline proof is not.
Compare it to the job requirement: Salesforce, pipeline generation, quota attainment.
Explain the visible rubric before the score: 37/100.

props.problemPunchline: A reviewer cannot search for what you meant to say.
props.teardownPunchline: The low score is from hidden tool and result proof in the rubric.
props.fixPunchline: Now the search term points to real evidence.

shorts.title: The job post gave the answer key
shorts.hook: The job post gave the answer key.
shorts.script: Job post: React, TypeScript, Next.js. Resume line: Worked on frontend features for internal and customer-facing tools. See the gap? The answer key is right there. The resume missed it, so it starts at 41. I would write: shipped React and TypeScript checkout work that reduced drop-off 14 percent. Now the tool, metric, and outcome are visible. That is why it moves to 90. Run the free Signal score before you apply.
storyboard: Case: Frontend Engineer Resume against Frontend Software Engineer.
Open on the job post with React, TypeScript, and Next.js highlighted.
Slide to the resume line and circle the mismatch.
Show the Signal Fit Score rubric before the score reveal.
Rewrite the line without inventing anything.
Reveal the rewritten bullet and then the score movement.
End with the free Signal score CTA.

The stack is probably real, but the bullet makes me hunt for it.

props.problemPunchline: The role language is visible. The resume is not using it.
props.teardownPunchline: Low score reason: broad work, missing tool, no result.
props.fixPunchline: Use the job language only when the experience supports it.

monetization.primaryGoal: Site revenue from resume / cover letter packages, not ad revenue.
monetization.cta: Free Signal score -> $9.99 resume or $14.99 bundle.
monetization.upsell: Promote bundle after score completion and multi-role pack after first purchase.

reviewComments.comment: Finally saw improvements in my resume after watching! Thanks, Signal!
The humor makes this process so much less painful. I laughed, I cried, and I definitely learned!

thesis: Turn a timely job-search pain point into one useful long-form teardown and several short proof clips.
